content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Members of the Commission                          Date : NOVEMBER 25, 197
     OK.  [handwritten names illegible] - Withdrawn

Add this to general consultation of gifts -

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION              77-11-279
       Natalie E. West

Subject: Gifts of food, travel and lodging received by the spouse of
         a public official.


     Judging from questions received by the staff,
it is a common practice for an organization to purchase
transportation, food and lodging for an official and for
the official's spouse. For example, a corporation may
ask an official to address the company's annual meeting.
The corporation will pay for the transportation, food
and lodging of the official and of the official's spouse.
Recently several justices of the Court of Appeal have
asked what reporting obligations arise from their par-
ticipation in a judicial conference. The justices and
their spouses received transportation to and food and
lodging at the event. The expenses were paid for by an
association of California appellate lawyers.

     Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18728 makes it clear that the official has no reporting
obligation by virtue of the benefits he receives at the
event.1/

     With respect to benefits received by the official's
spouse, however, the staff has advised officials that
the cost of food, lodging and transportation received by
the spouse would be reportable by the official. We have
recently concluded that this advice is inconsistent with

     1/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18728 provides in
relevant part:

     ... free admission, food, beverages and similar
     nominal benefits provided to a filer at an
     event at which he or she speaks, participates
     in a panel or seminar or performs a similar
     service, and reimbursement for actual intrastate
     travel and for necessary
     accommodations provided directly in connection
     with the event are not payments and need not
     be reported by the filer.

Members of the Commission
November 25, 1977
Page Two


the opinion requested by Ken Cory, 2 FPPC Opinions 48
(No. 75-094-A, April 22, 1976) and that the advice lacks
sound basis in policy. Consequently we would like to
change our advice and request your approval before doing
so.

The Cory opinion sets forth three criteria for
determining when a gift ostensibly made to the spouse of
an official will be considered a gift to the official
and will be reportable by the official.2/ In that opinion
the Commission specifically held that meals and trips
received by the Controller's children are bona fide
gifts to the children and are not reportable by the
Controller. Although not explicitly stated it is clear
from the opinion that the same conclusion applies to
meals and trips received by the official's spouse.
Transportation, food and lodging provided to an official's
spouse may not provide any direct benefit to the official
and under the rationale of the Cory opinion cannot be
considered to be gifts to the official.

In addition to the reasoning of the Cory opinion,
certain policy considerations weigh against making trans-
portation, food and lodging received by a spouse reportable
by the official. The Commission has determined that
when an official participates in an event it is unlikely
that he will be improperly influenced by transportation,

2/ Gifts received by the spouse of an elected
state officer are the separate property
of the spouse and do not have to be dis-
closed under Chapter 7. Sections 87100,
et seq.... However, a gift ostensibly
made to the spouse ... of an elected
state officer shall be considered a gift
to the official if:

1. The nature of the gift is such
that the official is likely to enjoy
direct benefit or use of the gift to at
least the same extent at the ostensible
donee; and

2. The official in fact enjoys such
direct benefit or use; and

3. There are no additional circumstances
negating an intent to make an indirect
gift to the official.

Members of the Commission
November 25, 1977
Page Three


food and lodging that incidental to the event. Consequently,
it would seem that if payments which directly benefit
the official are not required to be reported, payments
of the same type that, at most, indirectly benefit the
official should not be required to be reported.

     In spite of the reasoning set forth above, it
is somewhat disquieting that officials and their spouses
receive expense-paid trips and incur no reporting obligations
because the official speaks, participates in a panel or
seminar or performs a similar service. Taking the reasoning
to extremes, it is possible public officials and their
spouses and children could be the beneficiaries of extended
unreportable vacations merely because the public official
participates in a seminar as part of that vacation.
Consequently, we ask the Commission to consider the
issue. However, it is the staff's view that in light of
the Cory opinion and the policy which underlies Regulation
13728 the Commission should determine that benefits
received by an official's spouse are not reportable.


NEW:plh

STATE of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date : 6-24-77


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ted Prim

Subject: Conclusion of Opinion Request Meeting of June 20, 1977.

A judge asked whether travel expenses received in
connection with three types of events need be reported
pursuant to Section 82030 or whether they were excluded
pursuant to Section 82030(b)(2) and Regulation 18728.
First, appellate court judges were invited to attend a
roundtable discussion with appellate court attorneys.
The discussions took place over a three-day period and
the judges' wives were invited.

Second, the judge is an American Bar Association section
governor who makes recommendations on ABA policy.

Third, he sits on a nine-member commission which advises
a nonprofit foundation on issues concerning justice in
America.

The first situation would appear to be in the nature of
a speech, seminar, panel or similar activity pursuant to
Section 18728. However, the second and third situations
may represent activities different from those which the
regulation was intended to cover. Does the phrase "educational, academic or charitable organization" as used
in Section 82030(b)(2) refer to organizations which receive tax-exempt status as educational, academic or
charitable organizations?

CONCLUSIONS:

1. The event is a panel or seminar within the meaning of 18728 and
therefore the expenses may be excluded from the judge's income if they
constitute food or beverages received at the event, intrastate travel
or necessary accommodations. However, the spouse's presence on the
trip is something which is shared by the official and hence is reportable by the official pursuant to the Cory Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 48.

2. The terms "seminar or panel" for purposes of 18728 are to be interpreted broadly, but the terms "bona fide educational, academic or
charitable institution" as used in Section 82030(b)(2) are to be interpreted narrowly in the tax sense pursuant to Section 501(c) of the
Internal Revenue Code. Accordingly, the expenses may be excluded from
the judge's income pursuant to 18728 but not pursuant to 82030(b)(2).

3. Same as answer 2 except the travel in this case was interstate

sections.conclusion:
1. The event is a panel or seminar within the meaning of 18728 and therefore the expenses may be excluded from the judge's income if they constitute food or beverages received at the event, intrastate travel or necessary accommodations. However, the spouse's presence on the trip is something which is shared by the official and hence is reportable by the official pursuant to the Cory Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 48.

2. The terms "seminar or panel" for purposes of 18728 are to be interpreted broadly, but the terms "bona fide educational, academic or charitable institution" as used in Section 82030(b)(2) are to be interpreted narrowly in the tax sense pursuant to Section 501(c) of the Internal Revenue Code. Accordingly, the expenses may be excluded from the judge's income pursuant to 18728 but not pursuant to 82030(b)(2).

3. Same as answer 2 except the travel in this case was interstate
sections.question_synthetic: What are the reporting obligations for gifts of food, travel, and lodging received by the spouse of a public official, and are such benefits reportable by the official under California Political Practices Code?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Benefits received by an official's spouse (transportation, food, and lodging) incidental to an event where the official participates are not reportable by the official, consistent with the Cory Opinion and Regulation 18728. However, the spouse's presence on trips represents a benefit shared by the official and may be reportable under certain circumstances.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains two memoranda: (1) internal memo from Natalie E. West proposing to change staff advice on spousal gift reporting, citing Cory Opinion as precedent; (2) separate memo from Ted Prim documenting conclusions from an opinion request meeting addressing specific travel expense scenarios. Both address the same underlying issue. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections; extracted Q/C from substantive content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the reporting obligations for gifts of food, travel, and lodging received by the spouse of a public official, and are such benefits reportable by the official under California Political Practices Code?

CONCLUSION: 1. The event is a panel or seminar within the meaning of 18728 and therefore the expenses may be excluded from the judge's income if they constitute food or beverages received at the event, intrastate travel or necessary accommodations. However, the spouse's presence on the trip is something which is shared by the official and hence is reportable by the official pursuant to the Cory Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 48.

2. The terms "seminar or panel" for purposes of 18728 are to be interpreted broadly, but the terms "bona fide educational, academic or charitable institution" as used in Section 82030(b)(2) are to be interpreted narrowly in the tax sense pursuant to Section 501(c) of the Internal Revenue Code. Accordingly, the expenses may be excluded from the judge's income pursuant to 18728 but not pursuant to 82030(b)(2).

3. Same as answer 2 except the travel in this case was interstate

embedding.summary: This FPPC memorandum addresses whether transportation, food, and lodging expenses received by a public official's spouse are reportable gifts. The Commission reverses prior staff advice and concludes that such benefits are not reportable, aligning with the Cory Opinion and policy underlying Regulation 18728.
